figures.caption: Table 13 Hyperparameters. Hyperparameters used for GCIQL, HIQL, and HILP on the Push-T and Diverse Maze tasks.
Figure 8 Success rate as a function of test-time compute for Push-T. Left: Setting d = 50. Right: Setting d = 75.
Figure 9 Success rate as a function of test-time compute for Diverse Maze. Left: Setting D ∈ [9, 12]. Right: Setting D ∈ [13, 16].

Figure 1 Top: Hierarchical planning in latent space. A high-level planner optimizes macro-actions using a long-horizon world model to reach the goal; the first predicted latent state serves as a subgoal for a low-level planner, which optimizes primitive actions with a short-horizon world model. Solid borders denote ground-truth observations; others are decoder reconstructions shown for interpretability only. Bottom Left: Empirical coverage. Hierarchical planning improves success on non-greedy, long-horizon tasks across multiple latent world models. ∆SR denotes the absolute success-rate gain from hierarchy. Oracle subgoals use externally provided subgoal images; otherwise, planning relies only on the final goal. BottomRight: Planning efficiency. Success rate versus normalized planning time for flat and hierarchical planners; hierarchical planning matches or exceeds success with ≈ 3× lower planning time. Highlighted HWM results prioritize planning efficiency rather than peak performance.

Table 1 Zero-shot robotic manipulation with Franka dualgripper, including pick-&-place and drawer manipulation. Flat planner succeeds only when tasks are decomposed into greedy subtasks via manual subgoals, whereas HWM enables end-to-end execution from a final goal image.

Figure 10 High-level plans in DiverseMaze. The agent is shown as a green ball and the goal as a yellow star. The pink trajectory is generated by the high-level planner, while the teal trajectory is generated by the flat planner. In these trials, the high-level planner successfully produces a sequence of subgoals that lead to the goal, whereas the flat planner often struggles due to the increased search complexity of long-horizon prediction.

Figure 2 Hierarchical planning in latent space. A high-level planner optimizes macro actions using a long-horizon latent world model to reach the final goal embedding. The resulting first predicted latent state serves as a subgoal for low-level planning, where a short-horizon world model optimizes primitive actions to reach this subgoal.

Figure 11 Initial and goal states for all evaluation tasks. (a) Drawer Tasks: 7 subtasks involving opening and closing drawers at various positions. (b) Pick-&-Place Cup Tasks: 10 subtasks requiring the robot to pick up a cup and place it at different target locations. Each panel shows the initial state (left) and goal state (right) for a given task.

Figure 5 Success rate vs. test-time compute. We vary the planning budget by sweeping CEM/MPPI samples and iterations (see appendix D). Hierarchical planning achieves higher success while using up to 3× less test-time compute than flat planners. Left: Push-T success rate vs. planning time (d = 50). Right: Diverse maze success rate vs. planning time (D∈ [9, 12]).

Figure 12 Executions on pick-&-place and drawer manipulation with a dual-gripper Franka arm. Dotted red columns indicate subgoals inferred by the high-level planner and decoded for visualization. Rows representing failed episodes are labeled with "Failure".

Figure 6 Prediction error (L1) as a function of prediction horizon. For short horizons (≤ 1 s), the low-level world model is more accurate. For longer horizons (≥ 1.5 s), a single-step prediction from the high-level world model outperforms autoregressive rollouts of the low-level model, reflecting reduced error accumulation.
Table 10 Hierarchical Planning CEM hyperparameters for Franka and Push-T tasks. Pred H and h indicate prediction horizons for the high-level and low-level planners respectively.
Figure 3 The high-level world model captures longhorizon dynamics using latent actions encoded from chunks of low-level actions. It is causal and trained with a latent prediction loss (Eq. (1)).

Table 5 Comparison of hierarchical model-based methods across key design axes. We categorize methods by whether they support zero-shot planning, the observation space of the model, the training paradigm, and the hierarchical interface between levels. We denote “partial” zero-shot when a method generalizes within a fixed, reward-defined task distribution but remains fundamentally tied to reward supervision, and thus does not support task-agnostic planning. In contrast, HWM enables fully reward-free, task-agnostic zero-shot planning.
